Discord Bots I've Used (and the Security Risks You Should Know)

I help maintain my company's Discord server, and a lot of people have DM'd me asking which bots I use and how to invite a Discord bot into their own server. So here's a writeup as a share-and-record post. I won't go super deep into every config detail, and at the end I'll talk about Discord bot hacks.
Mee6 is hands down one of the most feature-complete Discord server-management bots out there. Their site even has Traditional Chinese localisation and a GUI for configuration.
Honestly, Mee6 is huge — it doesn't really fit in a single post. You could write a whole series on it, so I'm just listing the features here.
React to a message with an emoji and the bot auto-assigns a role.
Chat in the server to earn XP — hit certain levels and the bot auto-assigns specific roles.
When your YouTube channel uploads, the bot posts a notification in the server.
Tracks online member count and total member count.
There's also a timer feature for scheduling messages, but I don't use it.
Some features are Pro-only, but my company has the membership so we get everything.
How to invite: log in on the homepage and click the "Add to Discord" button.
The author of this bot seems to have gone quiet. You might want to use
instead — the workflow is basically the same.
I covered Invite-Role Bot in detail in
How to use a bot to auto-assign roles via invite link (article in Mandarin)
. The short version: it lets you generate invite codes, and
joiners through that code get a specific role assigned automatically.
It's super handy, and I have no idea why Discord doesn't ship this natively. One catch — if the user has joined your server before, clicking the invite link won't auto-assign the role again.
I jokingly call Carl Bot "the little turtle" because of its turtle logo.

Carl Bot is in the same server-management category as Mee6, but with fewer features and English-only. If you don't need everything Mee6 has, Carl Bot is a solid pick — most of the core features overlap, and some things that are paid in Mee6 are free in Carl Bot.
For example, Carl Bot's scheduled messages let you target specific dates and times. Mee6's timer can only schedule intervals (every X minutes/hours).
How to invite: log in on the site, then pick which server to add it to.
This one's specifically for "Community" servers, which have a special channel type called Announcement channels.
Announcement channels can be followed by other servers — when you post something, the following server immediately gets a notification.
But! By default you have to "manually publish" each message before followers see it. Auto Publisher Bot fixes that by auto-publishing for you.
One warning: if you publish too frequently, the bot can get banned, so Discord doesn't tag it as spam.

Needle Bot auto-creates threads. After Discord added threads, a lot of tech communities migrated to Discord because threads are great — but the catch is you have to manually create one. Needle Bot fixes that: configure which channels should auto-thread, and any message in those channels triggers a new thread, with the date as the default thread title.
The thread title is also customisable by the original poster. The interface is English-only but operation is straightforward.

One last reminder — when picking bots, try to pick ones with a lot of users. Discord lets third-party devs build bots so you can create a totally unique server, but you should pay attention to ratings and adoption.

That said, even widely-used bots have been hacked before. There was an incident with "CAPTCHA BOT" — a verification bot used by tons of communities — where the bot itself got compromised. Same thing happened to "tickettool".
You can read more about it in
Removing a bot from your server
Just in case, here's how to remove a bot you're worried about. Right-click the server name → "Server Settings".
Search for the bot's name, e.g. Mee6.
Done — bot's gone from your server.
Another important step — when you use a bot, sometimes you log in and grant the bot certain permissions. You should also revoke those.
You can see which permissions each app has access to.
A confirmation will pop up — click through.
Now the app can't access any of your permissions.
Same old advice: never click unknown links, never trust "free Discord Nitro" offers — they're almost always scams (8 or 9 out of 10). Stick to that rule, and also enable 2FA following
Don't let your account become a zombie bot — protect yourself and protect others.

I help maintain my company's Discord server, and people keep DM'ing me to ask which bots I use and how to invite Discord bots into their own servers. So here's a writeup of the bots I've used — at a high level, no deep configuration — plus a section at the end about Discord bot hacks and what to do if you're worried.
